coredata.dc:title: B, N co-doped carbon dots based fluorescent test paper and hydrogel for visual and efficient dual ion detection
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Jiali
Guo, Yuqi
Gong, Tianyu
Cui, Kai
Hou, Linrui
Yuan, Changzhou
coredata.dc:description: It is highly significant to develop water-soluble carbon dots (CDs) for ion detection in the environment via a facile and economical process. Herein, the organic molecules derived boron, nitrogen co-doped CDs (BN-CDs) with water-solubility are solvothermally synthesized towards dual ion detection. The BN-CDs emit bright tangerine fluorescence, and demonstrate a fluorescent quantum yield of 17 %. Besides, the obtained BN-CDs show a good linear relationship with Cu2+/Co2+ in a wide dynamic range of 1–8 μM, along with detection limits of 16 nM (Cu2+) and 24 nM (Co2+). The joint effect of dynamic and static quenching mechanisms is proposed as well for fluorescence quenching by Cu2+/Co2+. Furthermore, two instrument-free BN-CDs-based solid sensors (i.e., test strips and hydrogel beads) are fabricated to sensitively monitor both Cu2+ and Co2+. The paper-based sensors exhibit selective and sensitive response towards Cu2+ and Co2+ in a low-cost manner. The fluorescence hydrogel integrates comprehensive advantages of hydrogel and BN-CDs, which makes it not only conduct as efficient fluorescent sensors to detect Cu2+ and Co2+ but act as the absorbent towards removal of the ions. The simple yet reliable sensing technique developed here favor efficiently detecting and recognizing dual ions in environmental and/or biological samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: B, N co-doped carbon dots
Fluorescence
Dual ion detection
Fluorescent test paper
Fluorescent hydrogel

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst pubtype ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence sensing of Cu2+ and Co2+
Test paper for Cu2+ and Co2+ determination

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image and HRTEM (the inset) image, (b) Diameter distribution, (c) XRD pattern and (d) Raman spectra of BN-CDs.
(a) FT-IR spectra of BN-CDs. (b) Full XPS survey spectrum and high-resolution XPS spectra of (c) C 1 s (d) N 1s (e) O 1s and (f) B 1s for BN-CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum, PLE and emission spectra of BN-CDs. Insets: the digital photographs of BN-CDs aqueous solution under daylight (left) and 365 nm UV light (right) irradiation. (b) PL emission spectra with different excitation wavelengths from 430 to 540 nm.
(a) Effect of NaCl concentration on the fluorescence intensity of BN-CDs. (b) Photostability test of BN-CDs under 365 nm UV lamp.
(a) Effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of BN-CDs. (b) Reversible fluorescence response curve of BN-CDs between pH 2 and 10 (emission at 608 nm).

(a) Influence of different metal ions on the fluorescence of BN-CDs (black bars); The quenching effect of Cu2+ on the fluorescence of BN-CDs in the presence of other metal ions (red bars). (b) Photograph of the co-incubation of BN-CDs with different metal ions under UV light (365 nm) irradiation. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) PL spectra of BN-CDs with the addition of Cu2+ with various concentrations from 0 to 500 μM. (b) Relationship between fluorescence quenching values (F0 – F)/F0 and Cu2+ concentrations. The inset for the magnified plot at low concentrations of Cu2+ (0–8 μΜ). (c) PL spectra of BN-CDs with the addition of Co2+ with various concentrations from 0 to 500 μM. (d) Relationship between fluorescence quenching values (F0 – F)/F0 and Co2+ concentrations. The inset for the magnified plot at low concentrations of Co2+ (0–8 μΜ).
Fluorescence decay curves of BN-CDs, BN-CDs + Cu2+ (10 μM), and BN-CDs + Co2+ (10 μM).
(a, b) UV–Vis absorption spectra of BN-CDs in the presence of 0 to 40 μM Cu2+/Co2+. (c, d) Linear relationship between F0 /F and the concentration of Cu2+/Co2+ ranging from 1 to 8 μM.

(a) Visualization detection of Cu2+ and Co2+ using the fluorescent test papers prepared by immersing filter paper in BN-CDs aqueous solution. The photos are taken under a 365 nm UV lamp. (b, c) The linear relationship between the brightness and tested Cu2+/Co2+ concentration.
Photographs of SA hydrogel beads under (a) daylight and (b) 365 nm UV light, BN-CDs@SA hydrogel beads under (c) daylight and (d) 365 nm UV light.
Photographs of BN-CDs@SA hydrogel after (a, b) Cu2+ and (c, d) Co2+ adsorption under (a, c) daylight and (b, d) 365 nm UV light, respectively.
Concentration values of Cu2+ and Co2+ before and after absorbed by the BN-CDs@SA hydrogel.
Schematic illustration of the synthetic procedure of BN-CDs and its applications for Cu2+ and Co2+ detection.
Schematic of introducing BN-CDs into SA hydrogel, and adsorption and detection mechanisms for Cu2+ and Co2+.

head.ce:title: B, N co-doped carbon dots based fluorescent test paper and hydrogel for visual and efficient dual ion detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Material Science & Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Material Science & Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Two instrument-free boron, nitrogen-doped carbon dots based fluorescent solid sensors (i.e., test strips and hydrogel beads) are fabricated to sensitively monitor both Cu2+ and Co2+.

It is highly significant to develop water-soluble carbon dots (CDs) for ion detection in the environment via a facile and economical process. Herein, the organic molecules derived boron, nitrogen co-doped CDs (BN-CDs) with water-solubility are solvothermally synthesized towards dual ion detection. The BN-CDs emit bright tangerine fluorescence, and demonstrate a fluorescent quantum yield of 17 %. Besides, the obtained BN-CDs show a good linear relationship with Cu2+/Co2+ in a wide dynamic range of 1–8 μM, along with detection limits of 16 nM (Cu2+) and 24 nM (Co2+). The joint effect of dynamic and static quenching mechanisms is proposed as well for fluorescence quenching by Cu2+/Co2+. Furthermore, two instrument-free BN-CDs-based solid sensors (i.e., test strips and hydrogel beads) are fabricated to sensitively monitor both Cu2+ and Co2+. The paper-based sensors exhibit selective and sensitive response towards Cu2+ and Co2+ in a low-cost manner. The fluorescence hydrogel integrates comprehensive advantages of hydrogel and BN-CDs, which makes it not only conduct as efficient fluorescent sensors to detect Cu2+ and Co2+ but act as the absorbent towards removal of the ions. The simple yet reliable sensing technique developed here favor efficiently detecting and recognizing dual ions in environmental and/or biological samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of industrialization process, the sensitive detection of heavy metal ions (HMIs) and their efficient removal are becoming the most popularized research domains due to their serious damage to the environment and even human body [1]. HMIs are commonly produced during the production processes such as electroplate, printing and dyeing, machinery manufacturing, medical field, and battery materials [2], which can inevitably introduce toxic pollutants into local resources. Among various HMIs, the harmful and non-biodegradable Cu2+ and Co2+ can be bioaccumulated through the food chain, severely damaging the ecological environment and endangering human health [3]. An excess of copper intake is highly toxic to central nervous system, causing neurodegenerative disorder like Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s diseases [4,5]. Copper is not easily metabolized, and even low-dosage copper can cause kidney and liver damage, and several diseases [6,7]. Similarly, the intake of excessive cobalt salts can easily cause adverse effects related to myocarditis, pancreatic damage, gastrointestinal dysfunction, polycythemia and deafness [8,9]. Hence, various analytical strategies have been developed for tracing HMIs in multifarious samples, including atomic absorption spectroscopy [10], electrochemical sensors [1], chemiluminescence [11], enzymatic assay [12], and so on. However, these traditional methods are subjected to the drawbacks such as necessarily expensive instruments, tedious pretreatments, low efficiency, and sophisticated operation [9,13]. Compared to these methods, the fluorescence-based sensors are potentially attractive. Therefore, the efficient and low-cost synthesis of sensing fluorescent materials towards detecting and removing Cu2+ and Co2+ has become the research focus in the biological and environmental fields.

With the advances in fluorescent materials, carbon dots (CDs) have received extensive attention over the past years due to their unique merits, such as abundant carbon sources, small molecular weight, water solubility, good photochemical stability, low toxicity, favorable biocompatibility, facile functionalization, and multiple active sites [14,15], which facilitate them wide range applications in sensing,13 optoelectronic devices [16], bioimaging [17], catalysis [18], and energy storage devices [19]. Especially, the dopant with other heteroatoms, such as N, S, and P, can further regulate their chemical and physical properties [20–22]. And their fluorescent property can be greatly improved via the moderate doping with these heteroatoms [22,23]. Obviously, these heteroatoms demonstrate relatively stronger electronegativity than C atom, while the B atom exhibits slightly weaker electronegativity, and the doped B atom could result in a positive charge distribution around C atoms [24,25]. Thus, the co-doped B and N atoms may generate different energy level in CDs, and further improve their fluorescent property [18,26]. Nevertheless, a fluorescent sensor constructed with heteroatom-doped CDs should be able to specifically bind the molecule of interest and produce measurable spectral response in the emitting wavelength and/or intensity. Even if the use of fluorescent sensors can exempt the sophisticated pretreatment process, reduce the time consumption, and even qualitatively analyze target species, the solution-based fluorescent sensors still have to rely on the aid of a fine fluorescence spectrometer, which hinders their application for real-time and on-site analysis of target species, especially in the less developed countries or impoverished areas.

Inspired by the easy signal recognition by obvious color changes of the fluorescent CDs, the problem may be solved by developing visual fluorescent test papers to achieve the qualitative and semiquantitative assays with the naked eye. The tool-free feature of such strip-based sensing systems demonstrates their incomparable advantages of low-cost, portability, and versatility [27,28]. Jiang et al. synthesized dual-emission CDs (blue CDs and red CDs) and applied them to construct paper-based device for the semiquantitative, visual, and sensitive speciation analysis of lead ions in a real sample [27]. Li and her co-workers developed a highly sensitive and selective fluorogenic paper-based device for sensing Cu2+ in the biological samples such as urine, serum, and cytochylema [28]. However, CDs in solid state commonly suffer from self-quenching due to the aggregation-induced quenching effect, and they hardly remain remarkable fluorescence, sensitivity and selectivity as those in the solutions, thus restricting their wide applications in optical and optoelectronic fields [29,30].

To address these issues, the most efficient and acceptable way is to introduce the CDs in a polymer matrix [30,31]. Hydrogels, as a class of three-dimensional (3D) porous matrix formed by cross-linking of hydrophilic polymers [32], are commonly considered as an ideal matrix for CDs due to their considerable internal surface area, the good compatibility between CDs and hydrogels, as well as excellent passivation capability of hydrogels to the surface of CDs [33,34]. Thus, the physical cross-linker 3D network of hydrogels not only can provide a solid platform for CDs, and further improve the fluorescence of CDs and facilitate their recycling [34]. By virtue of the high density of metal ion-coordinating groups of hydrogels, it is reasonably to envisage that the CDs-anchored hydrogels may achieve dual goals of the instantaneous semiquantitative detection towards metal ions and highly efficient removal of these metal ions from water.

Motivated by the above-mentioned considerations, a facile solvothermal carbonization technique was developed to prepare novel boron, nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (BN-CDs) were synthesized from boric acid (BA) and p-phenylenediamine (PPD) as B and N sources, respectively, as schematically illustrated in Scheme 1 . The resulted BN-CDs exhibited bright and stable tangerine fluorescence with a quantum yield (QY) of 17 %, and they were applied as “on-off” fluorescent probe for the detection of both Cu2+ and Co2+. The quenching mechanisms were tentatively proposed as well. Furthermore, BN-CDs were developed to fabricate a paper strip with a colorimetric nanoprobe for simple, rapid, low-cost, highly sensitive, and specific detection of Cu2+ and Co2+. The fluorescent paper strips can be applied to visual semiquantative Cu2+/Co2+ in a real-time/on-site manner. In addition, the fluorescent CDs-hydrogels were constructed by imbedding BN-CDs into a sodium alginate (SA) hydrogel network with functionalities of easy handling, simultaneously recognizing and removing of dual metal ions. The sustainability and simple preparation of BN-CDs-based materials provide great possibilities for real-time, low-cost, and visual detection in the future.

The PPD and SA were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). NaCl, KCl, MgCl2·6H2O, ZnCl2, CaCl2, NiCl2·6H2O, CuCl2·2H2O, FeSO4·7H2O, BaCl2, CdCl2, FeCl3·6H2O, MnSO4·H2O, CrCl3·6H2O, CoCl2·6H2O, BA and ethanol were all purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemicals and solvents were of analytical grade and were used without further purification. Ultra-super water used throughout all experiments was purified by using a Milli-Q Plus system (Millipore).

The size and morphology of the as-synthesized CDs were analyzed by using transmission-electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) (JEOL JEM-2100, Tokyo, Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded on an X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku-TTRIII, Tokyo, Japan). Raman spectra were collected on a Renishaw InVia Reflex using a 532 nm laser as the excitation source. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy 5700 infrared spectrum analyzer (FT-IR, Thermo Company, USA) was used to record FT-IR spectra. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed by using an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Escalab 250Xi, Waltham, MA, USA). All fluorescence spectra were performed by a Shimadzu RF-6000 spectrofluorometer equipped with a Xenon arc lamp, and the excitation and emission slits were set at 5 nm (Shimadzu Corporation, Tokyo, Japan). The ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were recorded with a Shimadzu UV-2600 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Corporation, Tokyo, Japan). The fluorescence lifetime was conducted on a DeltaFlex (Horiba Jobin Yvon IBH Ltd., Paris, France). The remaining Cu2+/Co2+ concentration in the solution was measured by Inductively Coupled Plasma Optical Emission Spectrophotometer (ICP-OES, ICAP7200, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA).

BN-CDs were synthesized directly via a one-pot solvothermal method. Briefly, 100 mg of PPD and 100 mg of BA were dissolved into 20 mL ethanol, and stirred magnetically for 15 min. The resulting mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave (50 mL), and was heated at 200 ℃ for a period of 12 h in an electric oven. After cooling to room temperature (RT) naturally, the product solution was separated by centrifugation (10 000 rpm, 15 min) to remove large particles. To realize the separation and purification, the obtained solution was filtered using a 0.22 μm microporous membrane to remove insoluble precipitate and dialyzed (MWCO: 1000 Da) against deionized water for 12 h to remove unreacted raw materials at RT. During dialysis procedure, the ultra-super water was exchanged every 3 h. The purified BN-CDs solution was partially preserved in refrigerator at 4 ℃ for further use. The solid BN-CDs powders were obtained after vacuum drying.

The QYs of the resultant BN-CDs was calculated by comparing the fluorescence intensities and absorption values of BN-CDs solution with standard Rhodamine 6G (excitation wavelength: 490 nm, quantum yield 0.894, dissolved in ethanol). In order to minimize re-absorption effect, the absorbance of BN-CDs solution was kept below 0.05. The QY was measured based on the following equation:
QYC = QYR * IC/IR * AR/AC * (nC/nR)2.

where I refers to the integrated emission intensity, n represents the refractive index of the solvent, and A is the optical density measured on a UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The subscript “R” and “C” stand for standard with known QY and the sample, respectively.

Various pH values of the resultant BN-CDs aqueous dispersions are adjusted for the fluorescence tests. For a typical procedure, 10 mL of 0.1 mg mL−1 BN-CDs aqueous dispersion is monitored to the target pH value by using diluted HCl or NaOH solution. The fluorescence spectroscopy was employed after 3 min at room temperature. The corresponding PL spectrum was obtained upon excitation at 490 nm.

In order to explore the potential application of BN-CDs, the interaction with a large number of metal ions was examined in water and the ion recognition was monitored by fluorescence spectrometry. Na+, K+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Cr3+, and Co2+ were selected to investigate the effects of these metal ions on the fluorescence intensity of BN-CDs. At RT, 100 μM of Cu2+ or other cations were incubated with BN-CDs solution (0.1 mg mL−1, pH = 7), respectively. The linear range and detection limit of Cu2+/Co2+ were calculated by measuring the change of fluorescence intensity at various Cu2+/Co2+ concentrations ranging from 0 to 500 μM. The fluorescence spectrum of the solution (λex = 490 nm) was measured, and the fluorescence intensity at 608 nm was recorded. The detection conditions were kept the same as mentioned above. In addition, the fluorescence photos of metal ions mixed with BN-CDs aqueous solution taken under 365 nm UV light were recorded.

The non-fluorescent cellulose filter paper was immersed in BN-CDs aqueous solution (0.5 mg mL−1) for 2 min, and then dried in oven at 60 ℃. The as-fabricated test paper was dripped with Cu2+/Co2+ aqueous solution at different concentrations including 25, 50, 100, and 250 μM, and dried at RT. The test papers were observed under a 365 nm UV lamp.

The SA molecules can be easily cross-linked by cations to synthesize steady hydrogel beads [35]. Typically, the SA (1.0 g) was dissolved in ultra-super water (50 mL) at RT after magnetic stirring for 4h to gain a clear solution. Then, SA solution was gradually dropped into 150 mL of 4% (w/v) CaCl2 solution for 12h for hardening. The relatively uniform spherical particles with an average diameter of 5 mm were moulded. Thereafter, the SA beads were collected through filtration, thoroughly washed three times with water to remove surface residue. Some SA beads were fully immersed in BN-CDs aqueous solution (0.5 mg mL−1) for 2 h. The typical gel beads were named as BN-CDs@SA hydrogel. Hydrogel beads were added into 20 mL of 0.1 mM metal ion solution (Cu2+/Co2+) with sufficient reaction for 48 h. The concentration of the remaining metal ions in the solution was measured by ICP-OES. The adsorption process was carried out at RT, and pH of the solution was adjusted to neutral.

The morphology and diameter distribution of BN-CDs were characterized by TEM, and the corresponding results were shown in Fig. 1
a, b. Evidently, the as-prepared BN-CDs are uniform and monodispersed with a nearly spherical morphology (Fig. 1 a). The HRTEM image (the inset in panel) shows clear lattice spacing of 0.30 nm, corresponding to the (002) diffraction facet of graphitic carbon [7]. The particle size distribution histogram of BN-CDs is obtained by counting 100 particles (Fig. 1 b), which displays a size range of 5.0–7.5 nm, with an average diameter of 6.3 nm. The XRD pattern (Fig. 1 c) demonstrates the crystalline structure of BN-CDs. A typical peak centers at 2θ = 25.8°, which corresponds to the (002) plane of graphite, indicating that the BN-CDs had both graphitic structure and amorphous carbon [6,23,36]. The Raman spectrum (Fig. 1 d) presents two characteristic peaks at 1342 and 1560 cm−1, which are in correspondence with the D-band and G-band, respectively. The D-band is ascribed to the vibrations of carbon atoms with dangling bonds in the termination plane of disorder graphite or glassy carbon, while the G-band is attributed to the vibration of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms in two-dimensional hexagonal lattice. The intensity ratio of the D-band to G-band (i.e., ID/IG) is estimated around 0.94. The dominant strength of G peak indicates the dominant graphitic feature of BN-CDs [8,22].

The chemical composition and element analysis of BN-CDs were identified by FT-IR and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy XPS. As shown in Fig. 2
a, FT-IR spectra of BN-CDs exhibits characteristic absorption peaks at 3150–3450, 3000, 1580–1750, and 1510 cm−1, which are assigned to the stretching vibrations of OH/NH, CH, CO/CO, and CC, respectively. Besides, the peaks at 1200–1350 and 1120 cm−1 are separately attributed to B−O/CN and B−C stretching vibrations [24,37,38], which confirms the presence of N- and B-containing functional groups in as-prepared BN-CDs. The results above further verify that the N and B atoms have been successfully doped into the skeleton of the CDs [39]. Typically, the formed hydroxyl and carboxylic groups on the surface of BN-CDs, without further chemical modification, endow them with excellent solubility in water. Additionally, the surface state of the resultant BN-CDs can be further proved via XPS measurement (Fig. 2 b-f). The full survey spectrum (Fig. 2 b) displays four characteristic peaks of C 1s (285.8 eV), N 1s (399.2 eV), O 1s (532.2 eV), and B 1s (192.4 eV). The BN-CDs sample presents different percentages of the atom content: C 1s (66.5 %), N 1s (13.9 %), O 1s (14.2 %) and B 1s (5.4 %). The high-resolution C 1s spectrum (Fig. 2 c) shows five peaks centered at 284.2, 284.6, 285.4, 286.4 and 288.3 eV, which are attributed to C–B, CC/CC, CN, CO, and CO, respectively. As for the N 1s spectrum (Fig. 2 d), the peaks at 399.2 and 400.8 eV are assigned to NC and NH, respectively. The spectrum of O 1s is deconvoluted into three major peaks at 531.5, 532.2, and 533.1 eV, corresponding to the CO, CO, and B–O, respectively (Fig. 2 e). The B 1s spectrum (Fig. 2 f) demonstrated two types of B bonds: B–C (191.9 eV) and B–O (192.4 eV) [39,40]. Meanwhile, for the convenience of comparison, the relative contents of all chemical bonds are calculated and summarized ( Table S1 ). These results revealed that a large number of functional groups existed on the surface of BN-CDs, which is responsible for their unique PL performance.

The optical properties of BN-CDs were verified by UV–Vis absorption and PL spectra. The corresponding results are presented in Fig. 3 . Fig. 3 a profiles UV–Vis absorption, PLE, and emission spectra. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum demonstrates three characteristic absorption bands mainly centered at 246, 300 and 490 nm, respectively. Specifically, the strong peak at 246 nm is typically ascribed to the π–π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domains (CC) [37]. The obvious absorbance shoulder at 300 nm was attributed to the n–π* transition of the conjugated CO or CN band, revealing the presence of carbonyl and amino functional groups [41]. The broad low-energy absorption tail band around the range of 400–600 nm should be contributed by the surface states of BN-CDs due to the adherence of more conjugated functional groups to its surface. As seen from PLE and emission spectra, BN-CDs present optimal excitation and emission wavelengths at 490 nm and 608 nm, respectively (Fig. 3 a). The strong fluorescence of BN-CDs may originate from synergistic effect of the co-doped N and B atoms in CDs. Visually, BN-CDs aqueous solution is light yellow and transparent under the visible light illumination. By contrast, when excited under UV light (365 nm), the strong tangerine emission is evident (the insets in Fig. 3 a). When the excitation wavelength is ranged from 430 to 540 nm, the emission peak just shows a maxima red shift of 10 nm, suggesting the relatively independent of the emission wavelength of BN-CDs (Fig. 3 b). The excitation-independent PL behavior of BN-CDs further authenticates that the strong fluorescence depends upon surface states rather than morphology. Furthermore, the QY of BN-CDs is calculated as 17 % by using the Rhodamine 6G as a reference.

Even without any further surface passivation, the as-prepared BN-CDs possess excellent solubility in water, which may result from the –OH, –NH3 +, and –COOH groups on their surface. Apart from the excitation-independent PL behavior, the optical properties of BN-CDs under different ionic strengths or irradiation time with UV lamp were also investigated. The PL properties of BN-CDs at different ionic strengths are monitored in NaCl solution with concentrations varying from 0 to 1.0 M. Apparently, there are negligible changes in fluorescence intensity of BN-CDs when NaCl concentration was increased from 0 to 1.0 M (Fig. 4
a), suggesting their excellent tolerance to high ionic strength. Such character presages their potential application in biological analysis and bioimaging fields. As illustrated in Fig. 4 b, the fluorescence intensity of BN-CDs remains virtually constant after continuous irradiation of UV light for 3 h, indicating the good photostability. Furthermore, they are resistant to photobleaching, providing a stable fluorescence signal for high precision sensing measurements.

To explore the effects of the surrounding pH values on the fluorescence of NB-CDs, the fluorescence intensities of NB-CDs dispersed in different pH value solutions ranging from 4 to 10 were examined, and the corresponding results were shown in Fig. 5
a. Typically, the BN-CDs show a significantly pH-dependent PL behavior, where the PL intensities decrease with pH values lower or higher than 7. Obviously, a dramatic decrease in the fluorescence intensity of BN-CDs is observed by continuously reducing the pH value of the CDs solution from 7 to 2, which suggests that the H+ can quench the fluorescence due to the protonation of amino functional groups. Unlike the cases under the acidic conditions, the fluorescence intensity of BN-CDs only decreases slightly by changing the pH value of the CDs solution from 7 to 10. The pH sensitivity of CDs may be related to the reversible protonation/deprotonation of functional groups on the CDs surface [42]. The color of BN-CDs aqueous solutions with pH value range of 4–10 illustrate obvious change under the irradiation of daylight, which can be distinguished easily by the naked eye (the inset in Fig. 5 a). The phenomenon is used to the realization of dual-mode pH sensing. It should also be mentioned that the color change process was reversible. To evaluate the reversible behavior of BN-CDs upon switching between acid and base media, the fluorescent property is determined during the pH cycling process. As presented in Fig. 5 b, the reversible fluorescence response between pH values of 2.0 and 10.0 can be readily repeated for several cycles (emission at 608 nm) without visible perturbation. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of BN-CDs can be successfully recovered in near neutral conditions after several cycles, highlighting the good pH-reversibility of the resultant BN-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing of Cu2+ and Co2+
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to verify the selectivity of BN-CDs to Cu2+ and Co2+, the changes in fluorescence intensity were investigated after BN-CDs interacted with a series of common metal ions including Na+, K+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Cr3+, and Co2+. The concentration of each metal ion is set as 100 μM. As shown in Fig. 6
a, the PL intensity ratio (F/F0 ) of BN-CDs with the addition of various metal ions (black bars) present that Cu2+ and Co2+ significantly quench the fluorescence of BN-CDs. However, other metal ions has slight or even no substantial effect on the fluorescence of BN-CDs. Compared with the primaeval PL intensity, 90 % of fluorescence is quenched. In addition, the anti-interference tests were also carried out. When other metal ions co-exist with Cu2+ (red bars), Cu2+ can still efficiently quench the fluorescence of BN-CDs (Fig. 6 a). And the quenching degree is not affected by other metal ions, indicating that the excellent selectivity of BN-CDs to Cu2+. As for the coexistence of other ions and Co2+, the selective tests were also conducted, and the corresponding results were displayed in Fig. S1. As expected, BN-CDs also demonstrate high selectivity towards Co2+. Fig. 6 b shows the corresponding photograph images of the CDs solution under the irradiation of 365 nm UV light after co-incubation of BN-CDs and different metal ions. It is apparent that Cu2+ and Co2+ can almost completely quench the fluorescence of BN-CDs by the naked eye. The above experimental results reveals that the as-prepared BN-CDs have the potentiality to detect Cu2+ and Co2+ with high selectivity, and demonstrate their unique advantages in sensing field.

The sensitivity of BN-CDs was further assessed in terms of detecting Cu2+ and Co2+, and the PL intensity of BN-CDs solutions with different concentrations of Cu2+ (Fig. 7
a, b) or Co2+ (Fig. 7 c, d) ranging from 0 to 500 μΜ were monitored. In the measurements, all excitation wavelengths were set as 490 nm. As exhibited in Fig. 7 a and 7c, a continuous decrease in PL intensity was observed with the increase of concentration of Cu2+ or Co2+. The fluorescence color of the CDs aqueous solutions, as displayed in the insets of Fig. 7 a, c, becomes much darker with the increased Cu2+/Co2+ concentration under 365 nm UV lamp. Furthermore, the fluorescence quenching values (F0 − F)/F0 vary with the Cu2+/Co2+ concentrations (Fig. 7 b, d). In the low concentration range, a rapid decrease of the fluorescence quenching values is observed, followed by a gradual stable with the Cu2+/Co2+ concentration approaching 500 μM. By fitting the above linear relationship between (F 0 − F)/F0 and Cu2+/Co2+ concentration in the range of 1–8 μΜ, where F0 and F are the PL intensity of BN-CDs at 608 nm in the absence and presence of Cu2+/Co2+ (the insets in Fig. 7 b, d), the following equation of Y = 0.03115C + 0.00589, R 2 = 0.9937, can be obtained to detect the concentration of Cu2+ quantitatively. The equation of Y = 0.04042C − 0.02028, R 2 = 0.9912, is obtained by the linear fitting, where Y and C are (F 0 − F)/F0 and the Cu2+/Co2+ concentration. Meanwhile, the limit of detection (LOD) of Cu2+ and Co2+ are calculated to be 16 and 24 nM, respectively, based on three times the signal-to-noise ratio (S/N = 3) [6,37]. To compare the sensing performance of the proposed method with other reported ones in detecting Cu2+ and Co2+, the respective detection range and LOD were collected in Table S2 . Obviously, the sensing performance of the as-prepared BN-CDs is comparable or even superior to those of previously reported CDs, suggesting the potential application of BN-CDs in detecting and quantitating Cu2+ and Co2+ at trace levels without expensive instrumentation and time-consuming analyses. In view of this, real-water samples were spiked with standard solutions containing Cu2+ and Co2+ of various concentrations as per the standard protocol. As shown in Table S3 , the measured recovery rates are all between 96 % and 106 %. It proves that the accuracy of the standard curve is high and the detection result is credible. The above experimental results all show that the specific identification and detection of Cu2+ and Co2+ reaction system based on BN-CDs has excellent performance such as high selectivity, good sensitivity, rapid response, and simple operation, which makes the method expected to be extended to the detection of Cu2+ and Co2+ in actual water samples. Accordingly, BN-CDs as the sensing probe are featured with the high accuracy, which is sensitive enough to detect Cu2+ and Co2+ in the common fields.

Generally, the quenching mechanism mainly includes fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), photo-induced electron transfer (PET), inner filter effect (IFE), dynamic quenching and static quenching [15]. Concretely, the dynamic quenching is caused by the interaction between the quencher and the excited state of CDs. It exhibits that the excited state of CDs loses their excitation energy through the collision of the quencher based on energy transfer or charge transfer, and returns to the ground state. The static quenching can be attributed to the interaction between the quencher and the ground state of CDs. It means the formation of non-fluorescence ground state complexes between CDs and the quencher [13,38]. The quenching mechanism of BN-CDs is explored with determination of Cu2+ and Co2+, and a series of experiments and calculations were performed.

The technique of time-single-photon counting (TCSPC) is performed to compare the average FL lifetime of BN-CDs with the absence and presence of Cu2+ or Co2+. After the separate addition of Cu2+ and Co2+ (10 μM), the average FL lifetime of the CDs is varied from 6.21 to 5.38 and 5.47 ns, respectively (Fig. 8 ). According to the tri-exponential fitting, corresponding results are summarized in Table S4 . Obviously, both additions of Cu2+ and Co2+ have significantly negative influences on the lifetimes of BN-CDs. In the dynamic quenching process, there is a relationship between the fluorescence intensity and fluorescent lifetime (τ0 /τ = F0 /F). It can be noted that the lifetimes without Cu2+/Co2+ are longer than that with Cu2+/Co2+, and the ratio of lifetimes (τ0 /τ) is consistent with the above condition. The results here demonstrate that the quenching contains a dynamic quenching process for our case [38,43].

Furthermore, the UV–Vis absorption spectra of BN-CDs solutions are monitored with the addition of Cu2+ and Co2+, respectively, in which their concentrations varies from 0 to 40 μM. UV–Vis absorption spectra (Fig. 9
a, b) demonstrate continuous changes of absorption bands for BN-CDs solution after the addition of Cu2+/Co2+ ions. In addition, the intensity of absorption bands ranging from 400 to 600 nm gradually becomes even stronger with increasing the concentration of Cu2+, and the fluorescence color of BN-CDs aqueous solution varies from bright tangerine to dark brown ( Fig. S2 ). As a result, there are non-fluorescence complexes forming between surface functional groups of BN-CDs and metal ions in the quenching process.

To further gain relevant insight into the quenching mechanism of Cu2+ and Co2+, corresponding Stern–Volmer plots are presented in Fig. 9 c, d. According to the Stern–Volmer equation [44], i.e., F0 /F = 1 + Kqτ0 [Q] = 1 + KSV [Q], where F0 and F refer to the fluorescence intensity in the absence and the presence of quencher, τ0 the fluorescence lifetime without the quencher, [Q] the concentration of the quencher, Kq fluorescence quenching rate constant, and KSV the Stern–Volmer constant. Therefore, the quenching rate constants calculated for Cu2+ and Co2+ are 6.9 × 1012 (R2 = 0.9944) and 9.4 × 1012 (R2 = 0.9895) L/mol·s−1, respectively, which are both more than 2.0 × 1010 L/mol·s−1, indicating the occurrence of static quenching [39,45].

Therefore, the aforementioned discussions conclude that the fluorescence quenching by Cu2+/Co2+ ions are caused by a joint effect of dynamic and static quenching mechanisms. The probable quenching process is tentatively described as follows. The Cu2+ and Co2+ in the solution collide with BN-CDs when they are moving in the solution. At the same time, both the two ions also coordinate with the functional groups at the surface of BN-CDs, and non-fluorescent complexes are always formed [38,46]. In addition, the quenching speed of Cu2+ is faster than Co2+ at the same concentration. Especially, the quenching fluorescence of BN-CDs by Co2+ is more obvious for the case of low concentrations, while the quenching by Cu2+ is more distinct at high concentrations. Thus, it is speculated that the above phenomenon may be attributed to the coordination of surface ligands of BN-CDs with a larger amount of Cu2+, and the influence of ionic strength at low concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Test paper for Cu2+ and Co2+ determination
ce:section.ce:para: Encouraged by the excellent sensing performance of the as-prepared BN-CDs in solution, we further investigate the detection potential of BN-CDs in the paper-based sensor strips. Here, the cellulosic filter papers containing BN-CDs are simply fabricated by immersing the filter papers in BN-CDs solution. The reason why the filter papers are selected as the sensing platform is mainly ascribed to their versatile merits like wide availability, easy handling and portable, low cost, and light weight, which identify them as an excellent substrate for analytical chemistry. To this end, the obtained fluorescent test papers are conducted for the rapid and visual detection of Cu2+ and Co2+, and corresponding results were shown in Fig. 10 . For the semi-quantitative detection, the filter paper strips with BN-CDs exhibits bright tangerine fluorescence under the irradiation of UV light (365 nm). After dropping various concentrations of Cu2+ and Co2+ solutions on the rectangle strips at RT, respectively, the changes in the fluorescent behavior are acquired visually under the irradiation of 365 nm UV light (Fig. 10 a). It is clearly observed that the dropped areas in the test papers gradually and consecutively display pink to dark with the incremental of Cu2+/Co2+ concentrations from 0, 25, 50, 100, to 250 μM, and the color change from pink to dark pink even occurs at Cu2+/Co2+ concentrations as low as 25 μM. Every dose renders a discernable color by the naked eye. More significantly, the fluorescence can be completely quenched by Cu2+, and mostly quenched by Co2+, which are consisted with the aforementioned discussions (Fig. 6). That is to say, the fluorescent test papers possess the semi-quantitative analytical capability for the detection of aqueous Cu2+/Co2+ ions. To further achieve quantitative assay, the brightness level was analyzed based on the fluorescent test paper. Obviously, the brightness and Cu2+/Co2+ concentrations have a good linear relationship, which suggests its exact feasibility towards real-time detection by taking images (Fig. 10 b, c). Therefore, it can apply this strategy to realize rapid detection Cu2+/Co2+ in water by monitoring variations in fluorescence brightness. Especially, test papers are stable in the dark, and can store for at least several weeks. Thus, the fluorescent strip-based system provides a favorable platform to be used as a selective probe due to its cost-effectiveness, simplicity, stability and flexibility.

The solid-state fluorescence of CDs is particularly crucial for its applications in optoelectronic, sensing and environmental fields [47]. However, these applications are seriously restricted due to their fluorescence quenching in solid state, which mainly results from the aggregation of particles [48]. To address such issue, the commonly recognized and efficient route is to disperse the CDs in a suitable polymer matrix. The SA hydrogel is commonly considered as an ideal polymer matrix, which has many merits including natural non-toxicity, environmental friendliness, long-term sustainability, excellent biocompatibility, low cost, and high adsorption performance [35]. Unfortunately, the traditional SA hydrogel still has some defects in terms of intrinsic properties. Nevertheless, its performance may be improved along with the increase in adsorption sites and the number of functional groups complexing with HMIs. It has been reported to prepare novel functional optical nanomaterials by imbedding CDs into hydrogels via covalent or non-covalent bond [31]. With regard to the sensing performance of CDs and excellent adsorption feature of SA hydrogel, the fluorescent CDs/hydrogel will achieve the capability of fluorescent detecting to HMIs, and concurrently has a certain ability to remove these ions. For this purpose, we introduce the prepared BN-CDs into the SA hydrogel matrix to construct a solid sensing platform, which is expected to effectively observe optical detect and simultaneously gain adsorption removal of Cu2+ and Co2+. Fig. 11 manifests the photograph images of SA and BN-CDs@SA hydrogel under daylight and 365 nm UV light, respectively. Obviously, the obtained SA hydrogel beads have no fluorescence under UV light, however, they expectedly emit the bright tangerine fluorescence, proving that BN-CDs are successfully introduced into the SA hydrogel.

Furthermore, these BN-CDs@SA hydrogel beads are incubated with Cu2+ and Co2+, respectively. Upon the separate irradiation of daylight and 365 nm UV light, the digital photographs of BN-CDs@SA Hydrogel after incubation with Cu2+ and Co2+ are collected in Fig. 12 . Compared the smooth surface of original BN-CDs@SA hydrogel beads, some wrinkles and vesicular protrusions clearly appear on their surfaces after the adsorption of Cu2+ and Co2+ (Fig. 12 a, c), which may be caused by the entry of Cu2+/Co2+ into the hydrogel, or by the exchange of Cu2+/Co2+ and Ca2+ during the adsorption process [49,50]. The process inevitably leads to the change of the surface morphology of hydrogel beads. Especially, after incubation with Cu2+ and Co2+, BN-CDs@SA hydrogel beads demonstrate no fluorescence under the irradiation of 365 nm UV light (Fig. 12 b, d). Subsequently, the adsorption capacities of BN-CDs@SA hydrogel for Cu2+ and Co2+ are investigated via the ICP technique.

Fig. 13 demonstrates the concentration varieties of both Cu2+ and Co2+ before and after adsorption. Apparently, the removal ratio is estimated as 88.1 % for Co2+, and even up to 98.1 % for Cu2+, implying that BN-CDs@SA hydrogel simultaneously owns high adsorption capacity for Cu2+ and Co2+. The fluorescent quenching may be attributed to the formation of non-fluorescence complexes between Cu2+/Co2+ and the functional groups on the surface of BN-CDs [51]. Meanwhile, a number of functional groups on the surface of SA hydrogel can also serve as ligands for chelating Cu2+/Co2+, which endows the Cu2+/Co2+ with a strong affinity for the functional groups of BN-CDs@hydrogel. Thus, the as-prepared BN-CDs@SA hydrogel is expected to act as an ideal bi-functional candidate towards qualitatively detecting and removing harmful HMIs.

Based on the above results, a feasible fluorescence quenching mechanism of BN-CDs@SA hydrogel as well as their efficient adsorption effect towards Cu2+ and Co2+ is schematically presented in Scheme 2 . The SA hydrogel bears the functional groups including COO–, –OH, etc., which provide good contact sites for BN-CDs [3]. Thus, BN-CDs can be well immobilized in the SA hydrogel matrix due to the electrostatic attraction and hydrogen bonding between SA hydrogel and the surface functional groups (i.e. –OH and –NH2) on the surface of BN-CDs. Owing to these groups including –NH2, –OH and –COOH, some metal ions can effectively chelate with CDs/hydrogel [2,32,52], which makes BN-CDs@SA hydrogel to be effective in sensing Cu2+ and Co2+ and simultaneously provides convenience in their separation. The excellent adsorption performance of SA hydrogel towards Cu2+ and Co2+ causes the shortened distance of them to BN-CDs. Thus, the stable non-fluorescent complexes can form due to the effective chelation of Cu2+/Co2+ to the functional groups on the surface of BN-CDs. And the non-radiative energy transfer occurs, which involves the partial electrons in the excited state of BN-CDs to the d orbitals of Cu2+/Co2+ [7,36], leading to the strong fluorescence quenching of BN-CDs@SA hydrogel. Consequently, the BN-CDs@SA hydrogel displays sensitive fluorescence response to both the Cu2+ and Co2+, realizing the visualization of recognition and simultaneously effective removal from the solutions.

In summary, we introduced a facile and low-cost solvothermal method for efficient fabrication of water-soluble BN-CDs. The as-prepared BN-CDs have a QY of 17 %, and act as a fluorescent probe for the selective and sensitive detection of Cu2+ and Co2+ with a LOD of 16 nM for Cu2+ and 24 nM for Co2+. The joint effect of dynamic and static quenching mechanisms was proposed for fluorescence quenching by Cu2+/Co2+ ions. Furthermore, the BN-CDs could be embedded in the cellulosic filter paper to fabricate a paper-based sensor strip, which was performed to detect Cu2+ and Co2+. Thanks to the promising advantages of easy handling, light weight, easy incorporation into devices, simplicity, cost effectiveness, and environmental friendliness, such paper-based sensor platform provided a novel design strategy towards selectively probing other targets, More particularly, a novel fluorescent BN-CDs@hydrogel was successfully constructed by incorporating BN-CDs into the SA hydrogel. The resultant fluorescent BN-CDs/hydrogel achieved a rapid visual response and high removal ratio to Cu2+ and Co2+. With the development of the diversified solid platforms, the fluorescent multi-functional materials of BN-CDs held huge potential in ion detection and recognition fields.

Jiali Xu: Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Yuqi Guo: Formal analysis, Investigation. Tianyu Gong: Formal analysis, Investigation. Kai Cui: Formal analysis, Investigation. Linrui Hou: Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Changzhou Yuan: Formal analysis, Investigation, Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52072151, 52171211, 52271218), Taishan Scholars (No. ts201712050), Jinan Independent Innovative Team (2020GXRC015), and Science and Technology Program of University of Jinan (XKY2119).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2022.110047.
